what_input_feature: A byte stream that (a) is detected/handled as UTF-16LE so control reaches UTF16LEToUTF8, and (b) contains a little-endian 16-bit code unit c with (c & 0xFC00) == 0xD800 (a high surrogate, low byte 0x00-0xFF, high byte 0xD8-0xDB), reached while the conversion while-loop is still running.
why_winner_satisfies: naive_ngram4 resolved 10/10 trials and drove the branch true 37 times (T=37 vs L T=0), reaching depth-1 lines 534-545 that only the winner explores; the winning seeds carry a UTF-16 BOM-like prefix and 0x00-interleaved bytes (e.g. ff fe ff at 0x0b-0x0d, and 3c 00 3f 00 patterns) that produce surrogate-range code units inside UTF16LEToUTF8.
why_loser_doesnt: Plain naive resolved only 1/10, was blocked 8/10 with T=0 F=149 at the blocker and never entered lines 534-545; its single blocking seed is mostly ASCII/0x20 runs that never form a 0xD8xx little-endian code unit.
mechanism_attribution: naive_ngram4 folds the last 4 edge IDs into the map index, so a previously-unseen 4-edge path through the conversion loop registers as new coverage; this finer-grained reward retains the intermediate corpus states that progressively build the surrogate-producing byte arrangement that plain edge coverage saturates on and discards.

what_input_feature: The same UTF-8 BOM gate (in[0]==0xEF && in[1]==0xBB && in[2]==0xBF), but reached opportunistically: naive_ngram4 needs only the byte triple to appear, with the n-gram-indexed map rewarding the distinct 4-edge path through the BOM-detection cascade.
why_winner_satisfies: naive_ngram4 folds the last 4 edge IDs into the map index, so traversing the chain of magic-byte checks in xmlDetectCharEncoding produces previously-unseen 4-edge path tuples that are scored as new coverage, keeping more BOM-adjacent inputs in the corpus; it resolves 9/10 vs naive's 2/10.
why_loser_doesnt: plain naive uses a single-edge MaxMapFeedback, so the intermediate states walking the encoding-detection if-cascade collapse to already-seen edge buckets and are discarded, giving naive no gradient toward the BOM triple (blocked 8/10).
mechanism_attribution: naive_ngram4's NgramHook indexes the map by length-4 edge-path tuples, so the multi-step encoding-detection cascade yields new-coverage signal that retains more near-miss inputs, whereas naive's single-edge feedback gives no such retention.

what_input_feature: In XML_PARSER_CONTENT state, an input whose '<' lead bytes do NOT spell the CDATA opener '<![CDATA[' at the cursor (specifically cur[6] != 'T' on the '<![CDA...' prefix), driving the dispatch chain to its false side and into the sibling content branches (end-tag '</', PI '<?', start-tag '<X', '&' reference, char-data).
why_winner_satisfies: naive_ngram4 winner seeds carry well-formed element/tag content (e.g. '<a>m', '<dtd', '<link:' fragments) that reach XML_PARSER_CONTENT and dispatch to end-tag and PI handling; the per-role overlay marks lines 11728-11735 (END_TAG / PI transitions) as winner-only [W] and the CDATA branch false, and the blocker line shows W T=0 F=12 (always takes the false side).
why_loser_doesnt: naive loser seeds concentrate on CDATA-opener-like and DOCTYPE prefixes (e.g. '<![C' fragments), so they enter the CDATA_SECTION arm; the overlay marks lines 11755-11757 and 11813+ as loser-only [L] and the blocker line shows L T=21 F=1 — the loser takes the true side and stalls inside the unterminated-CDATA scanning logic.
mechanism_attribution: naive_ngram4's NgramHook folds the last 4 edge IDs into the map index, so a previously-unseen 4-edge path tuple counts as new coverage. The content-dispatch cascade (cur=='<' then the next/cur[2..6] tests) produces many distinct short edge paths as the false side fans out to END_TAG/PI/start-tag/char-data; the n-gram feedback rewards seeds that exercise these path tuples, keeping diverse non-CDATA content seeds in the corpus that resolve the false branch. Plain naive (single-edge buckets) gives no extra credit for the path context, so it under-retains those seeds and stalls.

what_input_feature: Identical program-side condition: a 3-byte UTF-8 lead byte (c & 0xf0 == 0xe0) with two valid continuation bytes decoding to a value < 0x800 (overlong 3-byte encoding), which takes the true branch (goto encoding_error) at parserInternals.c:622.
why_winner_satisfies: naive_ngram4 resolves the branch in 8/10 trials with hitcount 8 on the branch, reaching the same [B]-marked 3-byte decode block and the [W]-marked true side at line 623. All five winner-resolving seeds were stored by naive_ngram4 (e.g. seed id=0020c73cfac74f46, fuzzer=naive_ngram4), and they carry the byte material (e.g. 0xe0 0x99 0x99 runs) that forms 3-byte UTF-8 sequences. The N=4 edge-tuple index turns the path leading into the overlong-3-byte decode into a previously-unseen n-gram, retaining the seed.
why_loser_doesnt: naive blocks in 8/10 (L T=0 F=1730 at line 622) and stores the loser-blocking seeds (all fuzzer=naive). Its bare per-edge bucket collapses every route into xmlCurrentChar onto the same slots, so an input one mutation away from an overlong-3-byte sequence earns no novelty signal and is dropped before the bytes can be refined to val < 0x800.
mechanism_attribution: naive_ngram4's NgramHook folds a rolling history of the last 4 edge IDs into the map index, so a length-4 edge path through the 3-byte decode block registers as a new bucket; this path-sensitive granularity retains seeds that naive's single-edge feedback discards, supplying the corpus pressure to reach val < 0x800. This is ngram coverage, not comparison-solving.
